answer:
Several NotebookLM MCP servers exist, all functioning as bridges between the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Google NotebookLM's unofficial API [1][2]. These servers allow AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, and VS Code to programmatically manage notebooks, ingest sources, and generate artifacts (audio overviews, quizzes, and slide decks) [3][4][5][2].

While implementation details vary, most projects use a combination of the `notebooklm-py` library, browser automation (Playwright/Patchright), and a framework like FastMCP to handle tool exposure and auth via Google session cookies [3][1][2]. You can generally connect these servers to clients using either stdio or Streamable-HTTP transports [3][2]. To use one, install the chosen package, authenticate via the CLI, and register the server in your MCP client's configuration file [4][5][6][2].
